title: On the Relation Between String Order Parameters, Entanglement, and Dynamical Quantum Phase Transitions in Topological Dynamics
authors: Sirshendu Bhattacharyya, Szczepan Głodzik, Nicholas Sedlmayr
abstract: Topological order is defined by topological invariants, rather than symmetries and local order parameters. Nonetheless some topological phases can be characterized by string order parameters and entanglement. In this article we study how string order parameters and entanglement spectra behave out-of-equilibrium following quenches in one dimensional topological models with $\mathbb{Z}$ invariants. Previously it has been suggested that string order parameters could serve as an experimental probe of dynamical quantum phase transitions. Despite the existence of clear zeroes in the order parameters at critical times, we show that in general there is no exact quantitative or qualitative connection with the critical times of dynamical quantum phase transitions. Another possible connection is of dynamical string order parameter zeroes and dynamical crossings at the center of entanglement spectra. Here we see that there can sometimes be a connection, but it is not typical. Again there is no general quantitative or qualitative connection. Each dynamical form of criticality behaves independently, though we do see that critical times tend to be of the same order of magnitude and give an argument for why this is the case. We also find that a string order parameter which labels one topological phase can undergo non-trivial dynamics even following a quench between \emph{other} topological phases. We elucidate where connections can be made, and where they result from a consideration of insufficiently general models. These results cast doubt on the idea of genuine dynamical phases following quenches in such models.
summary: This paper shows that the zeroes of string order parameters and entanglement spectrum crossings do not generally coincide with the critical times of dynamical quantum phase transitions. While some coincidences appear in simple models like the SSH model, more complex topological models reveal that these different types of dynamical criticality are fundamentally independent.
main_problem: The study investigates whether string order parameters (SOP) and entanglement spectrum crossings can serve as reliable experimental probes for the critical times of dynamical quantum phase transitions (DQPT) following a quantum quench.
main_result: The authors demonstrate that there is no general quantitative or qualitative connection between the critical times of DQPTs, SOP zeroes, and entanglement spectrum crossings, showing that these forms of dynamical criticality behave independently.
method: The researchers use analytical derivations (Wick's theorem and Toeplitz determinants) and numerical simulations (diagonalization and finite-size scaling) to study the time evolution of observables after a sudden quench.
model_or_system: The study focuses on one-dimensional topological models, specifically the Su-Schrieffer-Heeger (SSH) model and the extended Kitaev chain with varying winding numbers.
key_observables: String order parameters (Ox, Oy), entanglement spectra (eigenvalues and crossings), Loschmidt amplitude/echo, and return rate.
important_parameters: Dimerization parameter (delta), chemical potential (mu), winding number (nu), and system size (N).
main_assumptions: The dynamics are studied under the assumption of an instantaneous (sudden) quench, and the models are limited to simple two-band chiral-symmetric systems.
figures_summary: Figures show equilibrium string order parameters, the time evolution of SOP and entanglement eigenvalues in the SSH model, and the lack of coincidence between critical times in the extended Kitaev chain.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of connecting different dynamical criticalities, presents the SSH model as an initial case, extends the analysis to more complex Kitaev chains to test generality, and concludes by discussing the independence of these dynamical phenomena.
why_interesting: This is highly relevant for many-body dynamics as it challenges the assumption that non-local order parameters or entanglement features can be used as universal proxies for identifying the precise timing of dynamical phase transitions.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1. Plots of M vs δ for the SSH model in equilibrium for N = 103. One can see that in the topologically trivial phase, where ν = 0, M = 0. For the topologically non-trivial phase ν = 1 we find M ̸= 0.

Figure 2. Plots of Mx vs µ for N = 1000 for the ground state of the extended Kitaev model with J = (1, 1, 1)J, ∆= (1.2, 1.4, 1.6)J. In the ν = −1 phase the string order parameter Mx ̸= 0 as expected. In all other phases it is zero. We note though that near the gap-closing point be- tween ν = −2 and ν = 0 finite size effects are large and Mx has still not fully converged to zero. In Fig. 3 we demonstrate the scaling of Mx for the representative points in the phase diagram we will use in the quenches.

Figure 5. Plots of M(t) for quenches in the SSH model within topological phases, as marked on the panels. The system size is N = 200. Small oscillations can be seen, but no zeroes occur and similarly there are no DQPTs, and hence no such critical times.

Figure 4. Plots of M(t) for quenches in the SSH model with |δ| = 0.4, as marked on the panels. The system size is N = 200. Vertical dashed black lines show the DQPT critical times and red dotted lines are plotted half way between the critical times as a visual aid. Panel (a) is the string order parameter rescaled by its maximum value, and panel (b) is the ln of the string order parameter. Zeroes are clearly visible in the non- trivial to trivial quench, ν : 1 →0 where δ : 0.4 →−0.4. The zeroes occur approximately at the DQPT critical times. The opposite quench shows small oscillations with a similar period, but no zeroes.

Figure 7. Plots of Mx,y(t) for different quenches, as labeled on the figures. Results are for the Kitaev chain with R = 1 and N = 350, see Sec. III A for details of the parameters. In- sets show Mx,y(t) rescaled by its maximum and main panels show the log to highlight location of the zeroes. Interesting to note in panel (a) is that both Mx(t) and My(t) have some interesting dynamics, despite the quench being ν : 1 →0, i.e. for two phase where Mx = 0 in both. See main text for further discussion. Vertical dashed lines show the DQPT critical times, with different colors referring to distinct critical momenta.
